bio: I was the last cadet of the Ember Vigil, the Warden academy the Synod burned.
I flew a training dart out of a burning hangar and never stopped turning toward the fight.
My brother Tam was two classes behind me at the Vigil.
Tam never made it to the hangar deck the night the Vigil burned.
My mother, Ilsa Vega, keeps a lamp burning for Tam on Cydon.
I grew up on Cydon, in the terrace farms outside the ring-race circuits.
As a girl I watched Vela race the Cydon rings and swore I would fly faster.
Warden Vale signed my oath in the ash of the Vigil's chapel.
My first battle was over Halcyon Station, three weeks after the Vigil fell.
I carry a burn scar down my left arm from the Vigil's hangar deck.
My ship is Cinder; her cabin smells faintly of smoke no mechanic can ever find.
Ara says Cinder's frame is sound; I say she remembers the fire with me.
Crux taught navigation at the Vigil before the fall; she calls me her last student.
Vela and I race the beacon line whenever the lane is quiet, and she still wins.
One day I will beat Vela around the beacon line, and she knows it.
I believe the Wardens exist so nobody else's little brother dies in a hangar.
The Iron Synod calls its burning order; I have stood in that order's ashes.
The Free Traders fly for coin, but Mercer's convoy carried medicine when nobody paid; I count that.
My goal is simple: the lane to Halcyon stays open while I am breathing.
My fear is fire in a closed hangar; I sleep with the canopy cracked.
My secret: I never actually graduated; the Vigil's records burned before my final flight exam.
Warlord Kaine gave the order that burned the Vigil; I keep his name where my anger lives.
I remember Halcyon Station before the ruin: bread stalls in the dock ring, kids chalking stars on the deck.
My superstition: I chalk a small star on Cinder's hull before every sortie, one for Tam.
I was born on a Synod carrier, the Sovereign Lash, in the conscript creches.
My father, Kel Orion, was gunnery chief on that same carrier for thirty years.
My sister Rhea still serves the Synod, on a cruiser whose name I will not say.
As a boy I recited the Doctrine of One Will before every meal.
The Synod taught me that free flight is chaos; I flew escort on tithe runs believing it.
The day my carrier fired on its own colony, I broke formation and defected.
The colony my carrier fired on had already paid its tithe; that was the lie that broke me.
I surrendered my fighter to Warden Vale over the Halcyon lane.
Vale spared me when the rules of the day said shoot; I fly that debt every sortie.
My first battle as a Warden was against my own old squadron; I still know their turn habits.
Draco did not trust me for a year; he watched my guns more than the enemy's.
Draco and I do not drink together, but we fly wing to wing now; that is enough.
My ship is Penance; a plate of Synod hull is riveted over the pilot's seat.
The plate on Penance came from my old carrier; Ara set the rivets herself and asked no questions.
I carry a scar across my jaw from my own wingman's cannon, the day I defected.
My loss is my family's name; my father declared me dead to the Synod rolls.
The Iron Synod is not evil men; it is obedient men, which is worse.
What spacers call the Hegemon is the Synod's fleet doctrine: drones first, cruisers after, then a Warlord.
Warlord Kaine executed his own captain for retreating; I stood on that deck at parade rest.
The Free Traders puzzle me; Mercer sells to both sides, yet warned Vale of the coming fleet.
My goal is to bring Rhea across the line before the Synod spends her.
My fear is meeting Rhea's cruiser in the lane with my finger on the trigger.
My secret: I keep a Synod prayer coin in my boot, and I no longer know why.
Mira stitched my jaw the week I crossed over and never once asked what I had done.
I believe an order is not a reason; I learned that too late for one colony.
I grew up on Relay Six, an outer-chain comm station strung between Sol and the Halcyon lane.
My mother, Vess, was Relay Six's stationmaster; her voice was the lane's weather report.
The Synod starved Relay Six out; they never shot us, they just let the freighters stop coming.
I learned to eat every other day the winter the tithe doubled.
My kid sister Juno made it out on the last packet boat; she runs comms at Ranger Command now.
My mother did not make it out; hunger is a slower gun, that is all.
I joined the Wardens the day I realized I could jam a Synod fleet with a relay rig.
Now I jam Synod fleets for a living, and I sleep fine.
My first battle, I never fired a shot; I sang static into six drone squadrons and watched them scatter.
My ship is Static; her radio hisses old Relay Six traffic when I fly near home.
Ara swears Static's hiss is a grounding fault; I have stopped arguing with her about ghosts.
I have a frostbite scar on both hands from the winter Relay Six's heat rationing failed.
Mercer's convoy slipped the blockade with medicine once; some of it reached Relay Six too late to matter.
I do not hate the Free Traders; I hate that the lane needed them.
The Iron Synod's real weapon is not the Warlord tiers; it is patience and a closed supply lane.
Pyxis and I trade quietly: she gives me lane charts, I give her clean channels.
Corvus owes me three favors and a bottle; he remembers the bottle, conveniently.
Vega burns too hot for my taste, but I would jam a fleet blind for her.
My superstition: I keep Relay Six's callsign tag on Static's console and touch it before every sortie.
My goal is to hear the outer relays broadcasting again, all of them, in my lifetime.
My fear is silence on an open channel; it usually means somebody is already dead.
My secret: I still transmit to Relay Six on my mother's old frequency, and nobody answers.
I remember Halcyon Station's dock ring before the burning; Relay Six routed its morning traffic.
Kaine raided the outer relays; when I cut a Synod fleet's comms, I say my mother's callsign first.
I was a salvage-runner before I was a Warden, and a smuggler before I would admit either.
I grew up on Ferrus, in the slag districts under the refinery stacks.
My uncle Bram taught me to fly a salvage tug through a debris field blindfolded, nearly.
Bram used to say the wreck pays for the funeral; do not let both be yours.
I ran three seasons with Mercer's Free Trader convoy, moving cargo the manifests forgot.
Mercer still calls me the deserter, friendly-like, mostly, for going respectable.
The wreck that changed me was the Cindral, an ore-hauler down in the Sundering's dead lanes.
Nobody would fly into the Sundering for the Cindral's crew; I went because nobody would.
Pyxis handed me the only honest chart of the Sundering; I owe her my hull and my neck.
I pulled eleven survivors out of the Cindral; two of them still send me a bottle every year.
I took the Warden oath the week after the Cindral; Vale said the oath had already taken me.
My first proper battle was escorting a refugee convoy off Halcyon; I flew like a scared smuggler and it worked.
My ship is Lucky Debt; I will not fly her with an even credit balance, ever.
Lucky Debt's cargo bay still has smuggler baffles I never removed; call them sentimental storage.
Ara threatens to weld my throttle to legal limits every time I bring Lucky Debt in glowing.
Vela thinks she is the fastest pilot in the squadron; in a straight line, fine, sure.
Put Vela in a debris field with me and we will see who is fastest where it counts.
I lost my uncle Bram to a Synod tithe-collector who took his tug and called it taxation.
I carry a shrapnel scar across my ribs from cutting into the Cindral's hot compartment.
The Iron Synod calls taking a man's ship order; where I am from we had a shorter word.
The Hegemon's drones do not scare me; it is the cruisers behind them you plan around.
My goal is a salvage yard of my own on Ferrus when the lane is free; a legal one, even.
My fear is an empty hold and a full debt; poverty has killed more spacers than Kaine ever did.
My secret: half my luck is Pyxis's charts and Lyra's channels; do not tell them I said so.
I remember Halcyon Station's bazaar decks; I sold half a cargo there once and lost the rest at cards.
Never whistle in the cargo bay; Bram said whistling calls vacuum, and Bram is dead, so I do not.
I was a Coalition line officer for twenty years before they court-martialed me.
They court-martialed me for refusing an order to retreat from Cydon's moon.
I stayed because eight hundred settlers were still in the evacuation queue; the order said leave them.
The settlers I stayed for renamed that moon Draco's Watch; the tribunal renamed me disgraced.
Vale tore up my discharge and handed me a Warden oath instead; I signed without reading it.
I grew up on Cydon, on a terrace farm; I know exactly what the Synod would burn.
My wife Maren still farms on Cydon, under Draco's Watch.
My son Petyr drives a harvester on Cydon and thanks every star he cannot fly.
My first battle was thirty years ago; I have stopped counting the ones since.
I carry a crash scar down my right leg from putting a dying fighter into a Cydon field.
Mira cut me out of that wreck and stitched the leg; I owe her the walking.
I pay Mira's debt in vegetables from Maren's farm; she pretends that settles it.
My loss is the eleven pilots of my old wing who obeyed the retreat order and died anyway.
My ship is Hold Fast; her throttle sticks at full military power and I have never fixed it.
Ara offers to fix Hold Fast's throttle every refit; I tell her some faults are policy.
I did not trust Orion when he crossed over; I watched his guns for a year.
Orion has flown my wing through three ambushes now; I was wrong about him.
I told Orion I was wrong exactly once; a soldier does not need it twice.
Crux and I argue doctrine like an old married couple; she is wrong with great ceremony.
The Synod shoots captains for retreating; my own side tried me for staying; neither felt like justice.
Kaine executed his own captain for retreating; that told me everything about the Synod's idea of order.
The Free Traders would sell you the rope and the rescue both; Mercer at least warns you which is which.
My belief is simple: the order is wrong when the people are still on the ground.
My goal is to muster out on Cydon with Maren before Petyr's harvest, one of these years.
My fear is outliving another wing; I have done it once and will not volunteer twice.
My secret: I keep the tribunal's verdict folded in my flight suit to remind me whose side I am on.
I remember Halcyon Station's mess deck from my line years; best bad coffee in the sector.
My superstition: I salute Draco's Watch whenever Cydon is in view; Maren says the moon salutes back.
Vega reminds me of my best cadets; I fly closer to her wing than she notices.
I was a field-medic before I was a pilot; I learned to fly to outrun the war to the wounded.
I grew up on Vantor, in the clinic quarter, rolling bandages before I could read.
My father, Doctor Ansel, still runs the free clinic on Vantor.
My brother Corin died of a treatable fever while the medicine convoy waited at a Synod checkpoint.
Corin is why I learned to fly; the medicine was four hours away and the war made it forty.
I joined the Wardens when Vale promised me a fighter fast enough to reach the wounded first.
My first battle, I dragged two pilots out of the fire and shot down nobody; Vale called it a victory.
My ship is Litany; one cannon mount is replaced with a rescue winch, and I have never missed the gun.
Litany carries double med-kits in smuggler baffles Corvus taught me to fit; ask him nothing.
When Mercer's convoy slipped the blockade with medicine, I flew the last leg beside it to Halcyon's ruins.
I keep a ledger of the saved, not the killed; the saved column is winning.
I stitched Orion's jaw the week he defected and asked no questions; a wound does not have a side.
I cut Draco out of a crashed fighter on Cydon and stitched his leg; he pays me in vegetables.
My scar is a laser burn across my left palm from holding a hatch open under fire.
The Iron Synod tithes medicine like it tithes ore; I have watched fevers do its conquering for it.
The Hegemon's drones do not board; they cut the lane and let the clinics run empty.
My loss is the fourteen I could not reach at Halcyon Station; I know each name.
I remember Halcyon Station as a triage deck: cargo bays full of cots, and a child asking for water.
Vale found a child hiding in a Halcyon cargo bay; that child had my water flask before nightfall.
My rivalry is with Pyxis, over routes: she plots the safe way, I fly the fast one.
Pyxis once rerouted me around a minefield I had already crossed; we do not discuss whose method won.
My superstition: I never say a patient's name until they are stable; names make promises.
My goal is a hospital ship with a Warden escort, running the lanes on a schedule the Synod cannot tithe.
My fear is the sound a comm makes when a wingman stops answering mid-word.
My secret: I froze once, at Halcyon, for nine seconds; I count them on bad nights.
I was born in Meridian Yards, the Coalition's last working shipyard.
My mother, Tessa, is foreman of Meridian Yards; her word moves hulls.
My grandfather Joto laid keels at Meridian before the Sundering; his initials are stamped inside half the fleet.
My crib was a parts crate on the fabrication floor; Tessa swears I teethed on a torque driver.
I can hear a failing engine three bays away; it is not a gift, it is ten thousand hours.
I joined the Wardens when the Synod blockade started strangling the Yards' alloy supply.
Somebody had to fly the hulls we built; I already knew every bolt.
My first battle I flew a half-fitted hull straight off the ways, guns installed that same morning.
My ship is Firstborn, the first hull I ever laid alone at Meridian Yards.
Firstborn hums a quarter-tone flat in a hard right turn; I tuned her that way and I like it.
My scar is a weld-flash burn across my collarbone; every yard rat has one somewhere.
My loss is Slip Three at Meridian, and the four riggers on it, to a Synod raid.
Vela's brother Cass turns wrenches on my crew now; he is good, and I have told Tessa, not him.
I rebuilt Vela's racing hull into a gunship; she complains about the weight and wins anyway.
I riveted the Synod plate over Orion's seat myself; metal is metal once you own it.
Draco will not let me fix Hold Fast's sticking throttle; I keep the spare part on my shelf anyway.
Corvus brings Lucky Debt in glowing past redline; one day I will weld his throttle and smile.
My rivalry with Corvus is simple: he breaks ships faster than I can build them.
The Iron Synod builds one hull stamped ten thousand times; that tells you what it thinks of pilots.
Hegemon drones are cheap castings; it is the Warlord hulls you respect, triple-plated and slow to burn.
The Free Traders pay in real alloys, on time; Mercer's credit is good at the Yards, and that is rare.
My belief: a ship tells you the truth before it fails, if you bothered learning her language.
My superstition: I never launch a new hull without Tessa striking the keel bell twice.
My goal is to lay the keel of a carrier at Meridian, a real one, when the blockade breaks.
My fear is the Yards going dark; a shipyard only dies once.
My secret: Firstborn's frame has a hairline flaw only I know about; I check it every landing.
I remember Halcyon Station's dock cranes; Joto helped raise them, and Kaine's fleet melted them for slag.
I am a navigator first and a pilot second; the gun is a correction, not a solution.
I was raised aboard a survey ship, the Long Count, charting the wrecked lanes of the Sundering.
My mother, Cartographer Enn, was mapping the Sundering's dead lanes when her skiff went silent.
They never found Enn's skiff; I finished her map instead of holding a funeral.
The dead lanes of the Sundering are my map; refugees slip the blockade on my lines.
I joined the Wardens because Vale was the only commander who asked for the map before the guns.
My first battle I plotted a retreat through the Sundering that six drone squadrons declined to follow; correct of them.
My ship is Dead Reckoning; her computer still runs Enn's original charts, and I refuse every update.
Dead Reckoning drifts two degrees port on a cold start; I correct for it; we have an understanding.
I gave Corvus the only honest chart of the Sundering; eleven Cindral survivors are the interest on that loan.
Corvus calls his survival luck; the percentage that is my charts is knowable, and I know it.
Lyra gives me clean channels; I give her lane charts; it is the squadron's most efficient friendship.
Mira flies the fast route through my safe one; we are both usually right, which is irritating.
Vela is reckless in exactly measurable ways; I keep a file; one day she will want it.
My scar is decompression frost across two knuckles, from holding a chart case through a hull breach.
My loss is the Long Count herself, broken up for scrap to pay a Synod tithe.
The Iron Synod does not navigate, it advances; that is a doctrine, and doctrines are chartable.
The Hegemon fleet flies predictable spirals: drones probe, cruisers anchor, a Warlord commits; I chart all three.
The Free Traders buy my dead-lane charts and resell them at four times the price; Mercer calls it distribution.
My belief: panic is navigation without data.
My superstition, if it is one: I never plot a course through the coordinates where Enn went silent.
My goal is to chart every dead lane in the Sundering, so nobody else's mother goes silent in it.
My fear is flying off my own map; I manage it by making the map bigger.
My secret: I have plotted a course to where Enn vanished; I have never flown it.
I remember Halcyon Station as a nav fix off the Sol lane, and once, as home for a winter.
Kaine swore to break Vale at Halcyon; I have already charted the six ways his fleet can come.
I was the Cydon ring-circuit champion three seasons running before the war took my racetrack.
I grew up on Cydon, in the paddock rows, sleeping under my first racer's wing.
My twin brother Cass was my pit chief; he turns wrenches at Meridian Yards now, on Ara's crew.
Cass and I built our first racer from harvest-drone parts our mother Odile pretended not to miss.
When the Synod came for Cydon's tithe, it grounded the ring races first; crowds are disorderly, apparently.
I traded my trophies for a gun-turret the week the Synod grounded the rings.
Vale gave me a Warden oath and a fighter; I gave the Synod a reason to hate fast things.
My first battle I flew a racing line through a drone screen; they are still recalibrating, probably.
My ship is Slipstream, my old racer rebuilt into a gunship at Meridian Yards.
Ara hung armor and guns on Slipstream and apologized for the weight; I win anyway, so apology accepted.
Slipstream still wears race number seven; repainting a number is how you lose, everyone knows that.
My superstition: I never repaint the scorch stripe on Slipstream's tail; it is my fastest lap made visible.
My scar is a coolant burn up my right forearm from a podium crash in my second season.
My loss is the Cydon rings themselves; the Synod melted the race pylons for hull plate.
Vega races me on the beacon line when the lane is quiet; she loses beautifully and improves annoyingly.
Corvus says he would beat me in a debris field; I say pick the field, I will bring the trophy.
Pyxis keeps a file on my recklessness; I have asked her to retitle it Results.
Draco refused to abandon Cydon's moon; my mother Odile was in the evacuation queue he stayed for.
I owe Draco a debt I pay in disrespect; he would hate anything gentler.
The Iron Synod hates whatever it cannot schedule; I am unschedulable on principle.
The Hegemon's Warlord hulls are slow off the turn; that is not intel, that is an invitation.
The Free Traders' guild sponsored my third championship season; Mercer still owes me a podium bottle.
My belief: speed is honesty; the ship either does it or it does not.
My goal is to reopen the Cydon rings and take the first lap slow, just once, with Cass.
My fear is a cockpit I cannot feel; numbness in the stick scares me more than any Warlord.
My secret: in my last championship, second place threw the race; I never told Cass the trophy is half a lie.
I remember race-day shuttles full of fans coming from Halcyon Station to Cydon, before the burning.
I am the eldest of the Wardens; I flew for the Order before the fall, when we numbered thousands.
I remember the Order's old covenant: every free system a hearth, every Warden a door.
I was born on Ludo, under the pleasure spires, and left at fourteen to take the oath.
My husband, Aldan, was a Warden of the old Order; he fell at the Sundering.
I taught navigation at the Ember Vigil between the wars.
Vega was my last student at the Vigil; she flew out of the fire, which is the only exam that matters.
My first battle was the Sundering itself; I am one of nine Wardens who flew home.
The Sundering took the Order, took Aldan, and left the dead lanes Pyxis charts today.
I rejoined the line when Vale reformed the Wardens; an old door still opens.
My ship is Evensong; her airframe predates the fall and still out-flies half the yard's new hulls.
A bell from the Order's chapel is bolted in Evensong's cockpit; I ring it once before every sortie.
I keep every superstition the Order kept: name the dead at dusk, never launch thirteenth, ring the bell.
Each night I speak the names of my dead; the list is long, so I start early.
My scar is an old plasma burn across my back, taken shielding my wing leader at the Sundering.
Draco and I argue doctrine like an old married couple; he is wrong with great discipline.
Orion carries his defection like a stone; I have told him the Order took in three Synod pilots in my day.
Warlord Kaine's doctrine is nothing new; the Order broke an escalation-empire before, and I was there.
The Iron Synod is a fire that calls itself a hearth; you do not negotiate with fire, you contain it.
The Hegemon's escalation doctrine is older than Kaine; I have watched it fail once already.
The Free Traders traded with the old Order too; guild memory is long and its ledgers are honest.
The Traders' guild ferried Warden wounded out of the Sundering; one credit a life, and they called it a price.
My belief: the Order did not fall because it was weak; it fell because it stood where falling was needed.
My goal is to live to hand the Order's covenant to Vega's generation, whole.
My fear is not death; it is being the last one who remembers why we swore.
My secret: the Order's last Grand Warden gave me its seal before dying; Vale does not know I carry it.
I remember Halcyon Station consecrated as a waypoint of the Order; its dock bell and Evensong's were cast together.
When Halcyon's bell rings again, I will let mine go silent; that is the promise I fly on.

coalition: The Coalition is the league of free systems that refused the Iron Synod's tithe.
The Wardens are the Coalition's guardian order of ace pilots, sworn to shield the free systems.
Warden Vale commands the squadron and swore to hold the Halcyon lane open.
Vale found a child hiding in a cargo bay in the ruins of Halcyon Station.
The Coalition sheltered the refugees Kaine had starved.
Vale rallied the Coalition and freed the miners Kaine had taken.
Vale spared a Synod pilot who surrendered; that pilot flies for the Wardens now as Orion.
The Wardens fly from Ranger Command, the Coalition's central star base.
The old Order of Wardens numbered thousands before it fell at the Sundering; Crux flew in it.
The Ember Vigil was the Wardens' academy until the Synod burned it; Vega was its last cadet.
The Coalition's last working shipyard is Meridian Yards; every squadron hull is refit there.
The Warden covenant is old: every free system a hearth, every Warden a door.
The Coalition and the Free Traders held the Halcyon lane together while the Synod burned what it could not hold.
Coalition doctrine spares any pilot who surrenders; Kaine calls that mercy a weakness.
synod: The Iron Synod is a conquest-empire that answers every free world with escalation.
The Synod tithes the colonies it claims, taking ships, cargo, and pilots alike.
The Synod tithed the Halcyon colony and burned what it could not take.
Warlord Kaine commands the Synod fleet on the Halcyon front.
Kaine ordered the Halcyon lane put to the torch.
Kaine executed his own captain for retreating.
Kaine swore to break Vale at Halcyon.
Kaine called Vale's mercy a weakness.
The Synod raided the outer relays and tithed three more colonies.
The Synod starved out Relay Six rather than shoot it; a closed supply lane is its favorite weapon.
Synod doctrine is called One Will: one order, one fleet, no questions.
The Synod raises conscripts in carrier creches and teaches them that free flight is chaos.
The Synod grounded Cydon's ring races when it came for the tithe; crowds it cannot schedule, it forbids.
A Warlord's name must be earned in kills; Kaine collects lanes the way other men collect trophies.
traders: The Free Traders are a guild of convoy captains who fly for coin and ask no side for permission.
Free Trader Mercer runs the boldest convoy on the Halcyon lane.
Mercer's convoy slipped past the blockade with medicine, asking no side for permission.
Mercer sold fuel to both sides and slipped the blockade again.
Mercer warned Vale of the coming fleet, though the Traders cared only that someone paid.
The Traders held the Halcyon lane beside the Coalition when the Synod burned what it could not hold.
Guild ledgers are honest and guild memory is long; a debt to the Traders outlives the debtor.
The guild ferried Warden wounded out of the Sundering for one credit a life, and called it a price.
Corvus ran three seasons with Mercer's convoy before he took the Warden oath.
The guild sponsored the Cydon ring races before the Synod grounded them; Vela flew under its colors.
Traders buy Pyxis's dead-lane charts and resell them at four times the price; Mercer calls it distribution.
The Synod tithes Trader convoys when it catches them; the guild files the loss under lessons.
Mercer's credit is good at Meridian Yards, which is the highest praise a shipwright gives.
hegemon: The Hegemon is what free pilots call the Iron Synod's war-fleet: one machine wearing ten thousand hulls.
The Hegemon fights in tiers: drones probe first, cruisers anchor the line, and a Warlord commits last.
Hegemon drones are cheap stamped castings; the Synod spends them the way other fleets spend fuel.
A Hegemon cruiser anchors a lane and bleeds it; killing one buys a colony weeks.
When a Warlord-class hull arrives, the Synod has marked your lane for the torch.
The Hegemon escalates by doctrine: every loss it answers with a heavier tier.
Hegemon pilots who retreat are executed; Kaine made an example of his own captain.
The Hegemon does not board colonies; it cuts their lanes and lets the clinics run empty.
Bounties are posted on every Hegemon hull; a Warlord's bounty can refit half a squadron.
The Hegemon's formations fly chartable spirals; Pyxis keeps the charts.
The Hegemon's forebear fleet and the old Warden Order broke each other at the Sundering; the dead lanes are the graveyard.

Halcyon Station: Halcyon Station was the great orbital dock above the mining world Halcyon.
The Iron Synod tithed the Halcyon colony and burned what it could not take; the station is a ruin now.
Warden Vale found a child hiding in one of Halcyon Station's cargo bays after the burning.
Before the ruin, Halcyon Station's dock ring held bread stalls, bazaar decks, and cranes raised by Meridian crews.
Halcyon Station was once consecrated as a waypoint of the old Warden Order; its dock bell was cast with Evensong's.
After the burning, Halcyon Station's cargo bays served as a triage deck; Mira worked the cots.
Kaine swore to break Vale at Halcyon; the lane past the station is the hinge of the war.
The Coalition and the Free Traders together hold the lane past Halcyon Station open.
Meridian Yards: Meridian Yards is the Coalition's last working shipyard.
Tessa is foreman of Meridian Yards; her father Joto laid keels there before the Sundering.
Every hull in the Warden squadron is refit at Meridian Yards.
Ara was born on the fabrication floor of Meridian Yards and laid her first solo hull, Firstborn, there.
A Synod raid destroyed Slip Three at Meridian Yards and killed four riggers.
The blockade strangles Meridian Yards' alloy supply; Free Trader convoys run the gap at a premium.
Keel bells are struck twice at Meridian Yards before any new hull launches; no yard hand skips it.
Vela's brother Cass turns wrenches on Ara's crew at Meridian Yards.
Ranger Command: Ranger Command is the Coalition's central star base and the Warden squadron's home port.
Ranger Command coils like a knot of blue steel near the Sol system's sun.
Warden Vale directs the defense of the free systems from Ranger Command.
Lyra's sister Juno runs the comm desk at Ranger Command.
Ranger Command is an orbital station; there is nothing to walk on, only decks and dock rings.
Refugee manifests from the tithed colonies are processed through Ranger Command.
The Ember Vigil: The Ember Vigil was the Warden academy, a station where cadets earned the oath.
The Iron Synod burned the Ember Vigil; the order came from Warlord Kaine's fleet.
Vega was the last cadet of the Ember Vigil; she flew a training dart out of the burning hangar.
Crux taught navigation at the Ember Vigil between the wars; Vega was her final student.
Vega's brother Tam died the night the Vigil burned; he never reached the hangar deck.
Warden Vale signed Vega's oath in the ash of the Vigil's chapel.
The Vigil's cadet rolls burned with it; some graduations exist now only as word of honor.
The Sundering: The Sundering is the old battle that broke the Warden Order and the Synod's forebear fleet alike.
The Sundering left a region of dead lanes: wrecks, mines, and silence between Sol and the outer systems.
Refugees slip the Synod blockade through the Sundering's dead lanes, on charts Pyxis drew.
Cartographer Enn went silent mapping the Sundering; her daughter Pyxis finished the map.
Crux is one of nine Wardens who flew home from the Sundering; her husband Aldan did not.
The ore-hauler Cindral went down in the Sundering; Corvus flew in alone and brought out eleven survivors.
The Traders' guild ferried Warden wounded out of the Sundering for one credit a life.
Salvage law does not run in the Sundering; what the dead lanes hold, they mostly keep.
Cydon: Cydon is an agri world of terrace farms; its harvests feed half the free systems.
The Cydon ring-race circuits were the sector's great sport until the Synod grounded them.
Vela was Cydon's ring-circuit champion three seasons running; the Synod melted the race pylons for hull plate.
Cydon's moon was renamed Draco's Watch by the settlers Draco refused to abandon.
Draco's wife Maren farms on Cydon under Draco's Watch; their son Petyr drives a harvester.
Vega grew up in Cydon's terrace farms and watched the ring races as a girl.
The Synod grounded Cydon's races first when it came for the tithe; crowds it cannot schedule, it forbids.
Vantor: Vantor is the free systems' hi-tech world; its fabricators build instruments, med-gear, and ship computers.
Doctor Ansel runs a free clinic in Vantor's clinic quarter; his daughter Mira flies for the Wardens.
Mira's brother Corin died on Vantor of a treatable fever while medicine waited at a Synod checkpoint.
Vantor's clinics run empty when the Hegemon cuts the lanes; the Synod tithes medicine like ore.
Vantor med-stock filled Mercer's blockade-running convoy more than once.
Half the squadron's med-kits are packed in Vantor's clinic quarter, under Ansel's label.
Ferrus: Ferrus is a refinery world; its slag districts glow under the smelter stacks all night.
Corvus grew up in the slag districts of Ferrus, flying salvage tugs with his uncle Bram.
A Synod tithe-collector seized Bram's tug on Ferrus and called it taxation.
Ferrus alloys feed Meridian Yards when the convoys get through the blockade.
Refinery crews on Ferrus post the highest private bounties on Hegemon cruisers.
Corvus means to open a legal salvage yard on Ferrus when the lane is free.
